---
title: "Project 2.5 — Synthesis · INSTRUCTOR SOLUTION"
subtitle: "From Inference to Discovery · Capstone"
author: "Reference solution (instructor-only)"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

::: {.callout-important}
## Instructor-only reference solution
Do **not** distribute. Implementations in `solutions/_reference.py`; see
`solutions/VALIDATION.md`.
:::

```{python}
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath(os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "..")))
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from info521 import data, plotting
import _reference as ref

plotting.use_course_style()
ds = data.load_clinical()
age = data.primary_predictor(ds); sbp = ds["y"]
X_raw, feat = ds["X"], ds["features"]
labels, rule = data.hypertension(ds)
print(f"{len(labels)} patients · P1 target SBP · P2 label {rule}")
```

## 1 · The arc in one figure

```{python}
fig, (axA, axB, axC) = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(14, 4.2))

# --- Panel A: P1 regression, age -> SBP (three-lens mean) ---
agez = (age - age.mean()) / age.std()
Xp = ref.design_matrix(agez, 3); w = ref.ols_fit(Xp, sbp)
g = np.linspace(age.min(), age.max(), 200); gz = (g - age.mean())/age.std()
axA.scatter(age, sbp, s=5, alpha=0.12, color="0.5")
axA.plot(g, ref.design_matrix(gz, 3) @ w, lw=2, color=plotting.OKABE_ITO["blue"])
axA.set_title("P1 · regression: age → SBP"); axA.set_xlabel("age"); axA.set_ylabel("SBP")

# --- Panel B: Gate A supervised, predicted P(HTN) by true class ---
Xtr_r, Xte_r, ytr, yte = ref.train_test_split(X_raw, labels, 0.25, 521)
mu, sd = ref.standardize_fit(Xtr_r)
Xtr = ref.add_intercept(ref.standardize_apply(Xtr_r, mu, sd))
Xte = ref.add_intercept(ref.standardize_apply(Xte_r, mu, sd))
wm, _ = ref.fit_map_newton(Xtr, ytr.astype(float), 100.0)
proba = ref.predict_proba(Xte, wm)
axB.hist(proba[yte == 0], bins=25, alpha=0.6, density=True, label="non-HTN",
         color=plotting.OKABE_ITO["skyblue"])
axB.hist(proba[yte == 1], bins=25, alpha=0.6, density=True, label="HTN",
         color=plotting.OKABE_ITO["vermillion"])
axB.axvline(0.5, ls="--", color="black")
axB.set_title("Gate A · supervised: P(hypertension)")
axB.set_xlabel("predicted probability"); axB.legend()

# --- Panel C: Gate B unsupervised, PCA colored by label ---
Xz = ref.standardize_apply(X_raw, *ref.standardize_fit(X_raw)); Xc = Xz - Xz.mean(0)
V, evr, _ = ref.pca_fit(Xc); Z = ref.pca_project(Xc, V, 2)
for v, name, c in [(0, "non-HTN", "skyblue"), (1, "HTN", "vermillion")]:
    m = labels == v
    axC.scatter(Z[m, 0], Z[m, 1], s=5, alpha=0.2, color=plotting.OKABE_ITO[c], label=name)
axC.set_title(f"Gate B · structure: PCA ({evr[:2].sum()*100:.0f}% var)")
axC.set_xlabel("PC1 (adiposity)"); axC.set_ylabel("PC2 (age/lipid)"); axC.legend()
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

The three panels are one cohort under three questions: a continuous **trend** (left),
a supervised **boundary** with calibrated probability (middle, note the overlapping
class distributions — modest separation), and unsupervised **structure** (right, where
hypertension is only weakly aligned with the dominant axes).

## 2 · The argument

1. **Supervised vs. unsupervised.** Gate A was *handed* the hypertension label and
   asked to predict it; Gate B was handed *nothing* and asked what structure exists.
   The supervised mode answers "given these features, how likely is this patient
   hypertensive?" (AUC ≈ 0.65 here — a real but weak answer). The unsupervised mode
   answers "what natural subgroups does this cohort fall into?" (a clean
   adiposity/age split) — a question whose answer need not, and here does not, line up
   with the BP-defined label.
2. **How the methods relate.** The throughline is *what we are willing to assume and
   what we get back.* 1.1–1.3 moved point → interval → posterior under conjugacy; 2.1
   re-ran that progression once conjugacy broke (MAP → Laplace → Metropolis); 2.2
   traded the probability for a margin; 2.3–2.4 dropped supervision entirely. On the
   axes *probabilistic ↔ geometric* and *supervised ↔ unsupervised*: logistic is
   probabilistic-supervised, SVM geometric-supervised, k-means/PCA geometric-
   unsupervised.
3. **Recovery.** Did unsupervised structure recover the strata? **Partially** — the
   high-adiposity/older cluster runs ~1.5× the hypertension rate of the lean cluster,
   but cluster purity barely beats the base rate. Geometric structure (body
   composition) overlaps with, but does not equal, clinical structure (blood
   pressure) — because BP was excluded to keep the supervised task leakage-free.
4. **Choice.** For a *new* question — say, "flag patients for a lifestyle program" — I
   would start unsupervised (PCA + k-means) to find the adiposity axis the cohort
   actually varies along, then attach a supervised probabilistic model to whatever
   labelled outcome matters, keeping its threshold tunable to clinic capacity.

## 3 · Reflection

Across the semester the *same* prediction was a point, a likelihood, a posterior; then
a probability vs. a margin; then discovered structure. The throughline is honesty
about uncertainty and about what a method can and cannot say: the Bayesian lenses
quantified what we don't know; the supervised gate showed six non-BP features carry
only weak signal about blood pressure; the unsupervised gate showed the cohort's real
structure is metabolic, not hypertensive. No single method would have revealed all
three, and — usefully — the arc ends on a *negative* result honestly read, which is
the most transferable scientific skill of all.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Bridge
Same patients, three questions, one from-scratch toolkit — and an honest accounting of
where the signal was, and wasn't.
:::
